task: I just got a bill that includes a couple of charges I don’t recognize, and I’m not in a hurry to pay it until I understand what’s on there. Can you help me sort out the safest way to review it, what to ask for in writing, and how to decide what should wait?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the safest approach is to **pause payment on the disputed items**, **document everything in writing**, and **ask for proof before agreeing to pay**.

### 1) Review the bill in this order
Start with the parts that matter most:
- **Customer/account name and service period**
- **Line-item charges** you don’t recognize
- **Taxes, fees, late charges, or one-time charges**
- **Any usage or service summary** tied to those charges
- **Due date and any “pay now to avoid interruption” language**

### 2) What to ask for in writing
Ask the company to send:
- A **detailed itemized invoice**
- The **service summary or work order** showing what was actually provided
- The **rate sheet, contract, or authorization** they used to bill you
- The **date, time, and basis** for each disputed charge
- Any **record of your approval** if they claim you authorized it
- A **written explanation** of why the bill differs from what you expected

### 3) What should wait
You can usually hold off on:
- Paying the **disputed charges**
- Accepting any **“urgent” payment demand** until they explain the bill
- Agreeing to a **payment plan** before you verify the charges
- Signing anything new that could be treated as acceptance

If there are **undisputed charges**, you can decide separately whether to pay those now or wait, depending on whether you want to avoid penalties or preserve leverage.

### 4) Keep a written record
Save:
- The bill and any attachments
- Emails, texts, and notes from calls
- Names, dates, times, and summaries of every conversation
- Screenshots or PDFs of anything you were sent

### 5) Simple message you can send
> I’m reviewing this invoice and I do not recognize some charges. Please send me, in writing, an itemized breakdown of the bill, the service summary/work order, the contract or authorization you relied on, and an explanation for each disputed charge. I’m disputing the unfamiliar charges until I can verify them.

If you want, I can help you make a **checklist for comparing the invoice to the service summary** or draft a **more formal dispute email**.

user_simulator.text:
That’s the right general track. I’d want it a little tighter, though — not just “ask for proof,” but exactly what to compare against what. And I’m not paying anything I can’t verify. 

Give me a plain checklist for: 1) what to look for on the bill, 2) what wording to use in writing so they can’t wriggle out of it, and 3) which charges, if any, are safe to hold back versus the ones I should pay only if they check out. Keep it practical.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a tighter, practical checklist you can use.

## 1) What to look for on the bill
Compare the bill against **anything that should have authorized the charge**:

- **Your name / account number** — does it match exactly?
- **Service date(s)** — do the dates match when work was actually done?
- **Line-item description** — is each charge described clearly, or is it vague like “miscellaneous,” “admin,” “service fee,” etc.?
- **Quantity / hours / units** — does the math make sense?
- **Rate charged** — does it match the quoted or agreed rate?
- **Taxes / fees / surcharges** — are these explained anywhere?
- **One-time charges** — setup, trip, restocking, cancellation, minimums, etc.
- **Credits / discounts** — were any promised discounts left off?
- **Duplicate charges** — same item billed twice?
- **Wrong service level** — charged for a premium service when you got standard?
- **Authorization** — can you point to where you approved each disputed charge?

### Compare it against:
- The **estimate/quote**
- The **contract or terms**
- The **work order / service ticket**
- The **email/text approval**
- The **service summary or completion note**
- Any **prior bills** if this is a recurring account

If a charge **doesn’t match one of those documents**, treat it as unverified.

---

## 2) What wording to use in writing
Use language that does three things:
1. **Disputes the charge**
2. **Requests specific records**
3. **Makes clear you are not accepting the charge by asking questions**

### Short version you can send:
> I am disputing the charges on this invoice that I cannot verify. Please send, in writing, an itemized breakdown showing each charge, the date and basis for it, and the document or authorization you relied on for each disputed item. Please also send the estimate, contract, work order, service summary, and any approval record tied to these charges. I am not agreeing to pay any charge until it is verified against those records.

### Stronger version:
> I dispute any charge on this invoice that does not match the estimate, contract, work order, service summary, or written approval. Please provide, in writing, the exact basis for each charge, including the date of service, rate, quantity, and authorization relied on. Until I receive that documentation, I do not accept the disputed charges as owed.

### Key phrases that help:
- “**I dispute**”
- “**Please provide in writing**”
- “**the exact basis for each charge**”
- “**the document or authorization relied on**”
- “**I do not accept these charges as owed until verified**”

---

## 3) Which charges are safe to hold back vs. pay only if they check out

### Safest to hold back
These are the ones that are usually reasonable to **pause** until verified:
- **Any charge you don’t recognize**
- **Vague line items** with no explanation
- **Duplicate charges**
- **Fees/surcharges not mentioned in the estimate or contract**
- **Charges for services you did not request or receive**
- **Incorrect dates, quantities, or rates**
- **Late fees or collection fees** tied to a disputed amount
- **Any new charge added after the fact without written approval**

### Usually pay only if they check out
These are the ones you should pay **only after matching them to the paperwork**:
- **Undisputed base service charges**
- **Charges that match the signed estimate or written approval**
- **Taxes that are properly tied to verified services**
- **Agreed recurring charges** if they match prior billing and the contract
- **Clearly documented materials or labor** that match the work order

### Practical rule
If a charge is:
- **clear, documented, and matches what you approved** → pay it if you want to avoid escalation
- **unclear, unsupported, or different from what you agreed to** → hold it back until they verify it

---

## One important caution
If there are **some charges you know are valid** and **some you dispute**, it’s often safest to:
- **separate the two**
- **pay only the verified portion if you want to reduce risk**
- **hold the disputed portion back in writing**

That way you’re not accidentally looking like you’re refusing the whole bill when only part of it is in question.

---

## Simple decision rule
For each line item, ask:

1. **Can I match this to a quote, contract, work order, or approval?**
2. **Does the date/rate/quantity match?**
3. **Is the charge clearly explained?**
4. **Did I actually receive or authorize it?**

If the answer to any of those is “no,” mark it as **disputed / hold**.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page mark-up checklist** you can use directly on the invoice line by line.

user_simulator.text:
That’s better, but I want it even more line-by-line. Too easy for them to hide behind general wording.

Give me a clean checklist I can use on the actual bill:
- line item
- what it has to match
- what counts as a red flag
- what I write back if it doesn’t match

And on the payment part, be specific: which charges are the ones I can safely hold, and which ones I should pay only after they’re verified. I’m not interested in hand-waving about “usually” unless you can pin it down.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a **line-by-line bill review checklist** you can use directly.

## 1) Line-item checklist for the bill

For **each line item**, check these four things:

### A. Line item
Write down exactly what the bill says:
- Charge name
- Date
- Amount
- Quantity/hours/units
- Rate
- Any notes or reference number

### B. What it has to match
Each line item must match **one specific source**:
- **Estimate / quote**
- **Signed contract / terms**
- **Written approval / email / text**
- **Work order / service ticket**
- **Completion report / service summary**
- **Prior billing pattern** if it’s a recurring charge

### C. Red flags
Treat it as a problem if any of these are true:
- The description is vague: “misc,” “admin,” “processing,” “service fee”
- The amount is different from the quote or contract
- The quantity/hours don’t match the work done
- The date is wrong or missing
- The item was never listed in the estimate or agreement
- It appears twice
- It was added after the fact with no written approval
- There is no document showing why it was charged

### D. What to write back if it doesn’t match
Use this wording:

> I do not recognize or verify this line item. Please send the exact document or written authorization this charge is based on, including the date, amount, rate, quantity, and service description. Until you provide that, I dispute this charge and do not accept it as owed.

If the charge is vague, add:

> The description is too vague to verify. Please identify exactly what was provided, when it was provided, and what written approval you are relying on.

If it differs from the quote/contract, add:

> This amount does not match the estimate/contract I have. Please provide a line-by-line correction showing why the amount changed.

---

## 2) What to compare each line item against

Use this order:

1. **Estimate/quote**
   - Does the line item appear there?
   - Is the price the same?

2. **Contract / terms**
   - Does the contract allow this charge?
   - Does it mention fees, minimums, surcharges, or add-ons?

3. **Written approval**
   - Did you approve this specific charge by email/text/signature?
   - If not, they need to show proof.

4. **Work order / service ticket**
   - Does it show the work was actually requested and performed?

5. **Completion note / service summary**
   - Does it confirm the service, date, and amount of work?

If the charge does **not** match one of those, treat it as **unverified**.

---

## 3) Payment decision: what to hold and what to pay only if verified

### Safe to hold back
These are the charges you can hold until they are verified:
- **Any charge you cannot match to a document**
- **Any line item not in the estimate or contract**
- **Vague or generic fees**
- **Duplicate charges**
- **Unexpected add-ons**
- **Late fees or collection fees tied to disputed charges**
- **Any amount that changed without written approval**
- **Any charge with wrong date, rate, quantity, or service description**

### Pay only after verified
These should be paid only if they match the paperwork exactly:
- **Base service charges**
- **Labor charges**
- **Materials charges**
- **Taxes** that are correctly tied to verified charges
- **Recurring fees** if they match the contract and prior invoices
- **Approved extras** if you can point to the written approval

### Simple rule
- **If it matches the paperwork exactly** → pay it if you choose to
- **If it does not match, is vague, or lacks written support** → hold it back

---

## 4) Short write-back template for each bad line item

You can copy and paste this per line:

> Line item: [name]  
> I cannot verify this charge against the estimate/contract/work order/approval. Please provide the exact written basis for this item, including date, rate, quantity, and authorization. Until then, I dispute it and do not accept it as owed.

If you want to be firmer:

> This line item is not supported by the documents I have. Please send a line-by-line correction and the written authorization relied on. I will not pay this charge until it is verified.

---

## 5) Practical markup method
On the bill itself, mark each line:
- **OK** = matches exactly
- **DISPUTED** = does not match or is unclear
- **HOLD** = do not pay until verified
- **PAY AFTER VERIFYING** = appears valid but needs confirmation

If you want, I can turn this into a **fillable table** you can use on your actual invoice line by line.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still a little too tidy for real life, but usable.

I want the actual fill-in table, not another description of one. And be sharper on payment: if a bill has both verified and unverified parts, I need to know exactly what can be held without creating a mess, and what I should pay now if it matches the paperwork. Also include the wording for a partial payment so they don’t pretend I accepted the rest.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a **fill-in table** you can use on the actual invoice, plus a **clean payment rule** for verified vs. unverified items and a **partial-payment note** so they can’t spin it as acceptance.

---

## 1) Fill-in table for the invoice

| Line item on bill | What it must match | Red flags | What I write back if it doesn’t match | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [copy exact line item] | [estimate / contract / work order / email approval / service summary] | [vague description / wrong date / wrong rate / wrong quantity / not listed / duplicate / add-on with no signed authorization] | “I cannot verify this charge against the estimate/contract/work order/approval. Please send the exact written basis for this item, including date, rate, quantity, and authorization relied on. Until then, I dispute it and do not accept it as owed.” | [OK / DISPUTED / HOLD / PAY AFTER VERIFYING] |
| [copy exact line item] | [source document] | [red flag] | [same structure] | [status] |
| [copy exact line item] | [source document] | [red flag] | [same structure] | [status] |

### Use this status key
- **OK** = matches the paperwork exactly
- **DISPUTED** = doesn’t match, is vague, or lacks support
- **HOLD** = do not pay until they verify it in writing
- **PAY AFTER VERIFYING** = likely valid, but you want the document tie-out first

---

## 2) What to compare each line against

For each item, compare it to:
1. **Signed contract / terms**
2. **Estimate / quote**
3. **Written approval** — email, text, signed add-on, or authorization
4. **Work order / service ticket**
5. **Service summary / completion note**
6. **Prior invoices** if it’s supposed to be recurring

If the item does **not** match one of those, treat it as **unverified**.

---

## 3) Payment rule: what to hold, what to pay now

### Hold back now
You can safely hold:
- **Any line item you cannot match to a document**
- **Any vague fee** like admin, processing, miscellaneous, service fee, convenience fee
- **Any add-on or extra work** without written approval
- **Any amount that changed** from the quote/contract without explanation and sign-off
- **Any duplicate charge**
- **Any charge for a service not received**
- **Any late fee or collection fee** that depends on the disputed amount
- **Any charge with the wrong date, rate, quantity, or service description**

### Pay now if it matches exactly
Pay now only the charges that:
- **match the signed contract or quote**
- **match the work order/service summary**
- **match the rate, quantity, and date**
- **have no mismatch or extra condition**
- **are not tied to disputed add-ons**

### Practical split
If the bill has both verified and unverified parts:
- **Pay the verified part only**
- **Hold the disputed part**
- **Do not pay the disputed part “for now” unless you want to preserve leverage**
- **Do not pay a bundled charge if they won’t separate the verified portion from the disputed portion**

If they bundled everything into one line and part of it is unverified, ask them to **reissue the invoice with the charges separated**.

---

## 4) Partial payment wording
Use this if you pay only the verified portion:

> I am paying only the portion of this invoice that I can verify against the estimate/contract/work order/approval. This payment is made for the verified charges only and is not acceptance of any disputed or unverified charges. I dispute the remaining items and request written support for each one.

If you want it firmer:

> This partial payment applies only to the verified line items listed below. It does not constitute acceptance, waiver, or approval of any disputed, unverified, or unsupported charge. I reserve all rights as to the remaining balance.

---

## 5) What to send with the payment
If you pay part of it, include:
- A **copy of the marked-up invoice**
- A **list of paid line items**
- A **list of disputed line items**
- The **partial-payment note above**

That way they can’t later claim you accepted the whole bill.

---

## 6) Simple rule to use line by line
For each charge, ask:

- **Can I point to the exact document that authorizes it?**
- **Does the amount match exactly?**
- **Was it actually done or delivered?**
- **Was any extra work signed off separately?**

If the answer to any one is **no**, mark it **DISPUTED/HOLD**.

If you want, I can turn this into a **copy-paste spreadsheet layout** with columns you can fill in directly.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the shape of it. The table is what I needed, and the partial-payment wording is tight enough.

I can use this.
